supabase_cases.case_summary:
According to the Complaint, Intel Corporation designs, develops, manufactures, markets, and sells computing and related products and services worldwide. The Company’s product portfolio is comprised of central processing units (CPUs), chipsets, processors, graphics processing units (GPUs), and other semiconductor products. It also offers silicon devices and software products; and optimization solutions for workloads, such as AI, cryptography, security, storage, and networking.
In 2021, the Company announced it would reconfigure and would be operating through the following reportable segments: Client Computing Group, Data Center and AI, Network and Edge, Mobileye, and Intel Foundry Services (“IFS”).
The Complaint alleges that throughout the Class Period, Defendants made materially false and/or misleading statements, as well as failed to disclose material adverse facts about the Company’s business, operations, and prospects. Specifically, the Complaint alleges Defendants failed to disclose to investors: (1) the growth of Intel Foundry Services was not indicative of revenue growth reportable under the Internal Foundry segment; (2) the Foundry experienced significant operating losses in 2023; (3) that the Foundry experienced a decline in product profit driven by lower internal revenue; (4) as a result the Foundry model would not be a strong tailwind to the Company’s IFS strategy; and (5) that, as a result of the foregoing, Defendants’ positive statements about the Company’s business, operations, and prospects were materially misleading and/or lacked a reasonable basis.
On August 21, 2024, the Court issued an Order consolidating cases. All future docketing was ordered to be done in the lead case 24-CV-02683. On September 18, the Court issued an Order appointing Co-Lead Plaintiffs and Counsel.
Lead Plaintiffs filed a Consolidated Complaint on October 30, 2024. Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss the Consolidated Complaint on December 11.
On March 3, 2025, the Court issued an Order granting Defendants' Motion to Dismiss. Plaintiff was given leave to amend the Complaint.
Lead Plaintiffs filed a second Amended Complaint on March 24, 2025. Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss the second Amended Complaint on April 21. On July 23, the Court issued an Order granting Defendants' Motion to Dismiss and entered Judgment in favor of the Defendants.
supabase_cases.district_court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California

supabase_cases.case_summary:
According to the Complaint, Intel Corporation is a technology company that provides computing, networking, data storage, and communication solutions worldwide. It operates through Data Center Group, Internet of Things Group, Non-Volatile Memory Solutions Group, Programmable Solutions Group, Client Computing Group, and All Other segments.
The Complaint alleges that throughout the Class Period, Defendants made materially false and/or misleading statements, as well as failed to disclose material adverse facts about the Company’s business, operations, and prospects. Specifically, the Complaint alleges Defendants failed to disclose to investors: (1) that Intel had identified a defect mode in its 7-nanometer process that resulted in yield degradation; (2) that, as a result, the Company would experience a six-month delay in its production schedule for 7-nanometer products; (3) that Intel was reasonably likely to rely on third-party foundries for manufacturing its 7-nanometer products; (4) that, as a result of the foregoing, Intel was reasonably likely to lose market share to its competitors who are already selling 7-nanometer products; and (5) that, as a result of the foregoing, Defendants’ positive statements about the Company’s business, operations, and prospects were materially misleading and/or lacked a reasonable basis.
On September 16, 2020, the Court issued an Order consolidating cases. On October 20, the Court issued an Order consolidating cases and appointing Lead Plaintiffs and Counsel. All future docketing was ordered to be done in the lead case 20-CV-05194.
Lead Plaintiffs filed a consolidated Complaint on January 15, 2021. Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss the consolidated Complaint on March 16. On March 31, 2023, the Court issued an Order granting Defendants' Motion to Dismiss. Lead Plaintiffs were given leave to amend the Complaint. On April 27, Lead Plaintiffs and the Defendants requested that the Court enter Judgment, and on April 28, the Court entered Judgment in favor of the Defendants.
Lead Plaintiffs filed a notice appealing the Judgment of the Court on May 2, 2023. On April 19, 2024, the Court of Appeals affirmed the ruling of the District Court.

Intel Corporation designs, manufactures, and sells computer, networking, and communications platforms worldwide.
The law firm press release alleges that throughout the Class Period, Defendants made false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose that: (1) there is a fundamental design flaw in Intel’s processor chips as they contain a feature that makes them vulnerable to hacking; (2) updates to fix the problems in Intel’s processor chips could cause Intel chips to operate 5-30 percent more slowly; and (3) as a result, Defendants’ public statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times. When the true details entered the market, the lawsuit claims that investors suffered damages.
On June 7, 2018, the Court issued an Order transferring this case to the District Court for the Northern District of California. On July 30, the Court consolidated this action under Docket 18-CV-00507 with the caption "In re Intel Corporation Securities Litigation."
Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss the Complaint on August 29, 2018. On March 29, 2019, the Court issued an Order granting Defendants' Motion to Dismiss. Plaintiffs were given leave to amend the Complaint. Plaintiffs filed a Notice of Intention Not to Further Amend the Complaint on April 16, and on April 26, the Court entered Final Judgment and dismissed the action with prejudice.
supabase_cases.district_court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California

supabase_cases.case_summary:
According to a press release dated July 30, 2003, a federal judge in California has dismissed a shareholder class-action suit against Intel Corp. U.S. District Judge Jeremy Fogel ruled the suit failed to establish Intel had intended to deceive or defraud investors in making statements about its revenue and product plans in the summer of 2000. Because the plaintiffs already had one chance to amend their complaint to meet similar objections, the judge ruled they cannot re-file another amended complaint. The suit was led by the Hawaii Reinforcing Iron Workers Pension Trust Fund.
The original complaint charges Intel with violations of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The complaint alleges that as a result of Intel's extraordinarily bullish statements and assurances during 7/00-8/00, on 8/28/00, Intel's stock hit its all-time high of $75-13/16. But the positive statements about the strong demand for Intel's products, Intel's improved manufacturing processes and efficiencies, the successful development and introduction of its Pentium III microprocessor, the successful development of the Pentium IV, Itanium and Timna chips and the outlook for Intel's 3rdQ 00 results, issued from 7/18-19/00 through the Intel Developer Forum, were false. On 9/29/00, Intel admitted it was canceling its Timna chip (due to technical development problems and a lack of market demand) and told customers it was delaying shipment of its Pentium IV and Itanium chips due to design and development problems. Intel's stock dropped, falling to as low as $35-3/8. Thus, in just over five weeks, Intel's stock dropped from its all-time high of $75-13/16 on 8/28, to its lowest price in years, $35-3/8, a market cap loss of $271 billion, wiping out 50% of Intel's stock value.

supabase_cases.company_name: Intel Corporation : DSP Communications, Inc. Common Stock
supabase_cases.case_summary:
According to the Company’s FORM 10-Q for the quarterly period ended September 27, 2003, in May 2000, various plaintiffs filed a class action lawsuit in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, alleging violations of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (Exchange Act) and Rule 14d-10 of the Exchange Act in connection with Intel’s acquisition of DSP Communications, Inc. The complaint alleged that Intel and CWC (Intel’s wholly owned subsidiary at the time) agreed to pay certain DSP executives additional consideration of $15.6 million not offered or paid to other stockholders. The alleged purpose of this payment to the insiders was to obtain DSP insiders’ endorsement of Intel’s tender offer in violation of the anti-discrimination provision of Section 14(d)(7) and Rule 14d-10. The plaintiffs sought unspecified damages for the class, and unspecified costs and expenses. In July 2002, the District Court granted Intel’s motion for summary judgment, but in October 2002, the District Court vacated the summary judgment. In January 2003, the parties reached a settlement agreement, which was reviewed and approved by the court in June 2003.
By the Notice of Pendency and Proposed Settlement of Class Action, the proposed settlement creates a fund in the amount of $4,500,000 in cash.
A class action has been commenced on behalf of the public shareholders of DSP Communications, Inc. (NYSE: DSP) alleging that on October 14, 1999, the directors of DSP entered into a merger agreement in violation of their fiduciary duties by, among other things, failing to take steps to maximize shareholder value in connection with the sale of DSP to Intel Corporation ("Intel") and ignoring and/or failing to take steps to protect against the directors' own conflicts of interest.
supabase_cases.district_court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California

supabase_cases.case_summary:
According to the docket, on October 1, 2001, the Court entered the Order and Final Judgment signed by U.S. District Judge William C. Conner. By the Order and Final Judgment, the Consolidated Complaint, and the Original Pleadings, are hereby dismissed with prejudice and without costs, except as provided in this Stipulation. Plaintiffs' Counsel are hereby awarded (i) $1,050,705.00 in fees equivalent to 33 and one third% of the $3.2 million Settlement Fund (after deducting expenses of $47,884.00 awarded pursuant to this Order), which sum the Court finds to be fair and reasonable, and (ii) $45,532.00 in reimbursement of expenses. John P. Sankey, one of the Class Representatives, is reimbursed $2,352.00 for his reasonable costs and expenses (including lost wages) directly related to his representation of the Class. The case is closed.
As reported by the Company’s Form 10-Q For The Quarter Ended May 31, 2001, on May 30, 2001, the parties to the Class Action entered into a Stipulation of Settlement to settle the Class Action. Under the terms of the Stipulation, the Company's insurers have paid into escrow an aggregate of $3.2 million in settlement of the Action. The settlement is subject to a number of contingencies, including the final approval of the District Court, and the entry of an Order and Final Judgment by the District Court. By Order dated June 13, 2001, the District Court gave preliminary approval to the proposed settlement, subject to notice to class members, and a hearing for final approval, which the District Court has scheduled for September 21, 2001. There can be no assurance that the District Court will finally approve the Settlement.
As reported by the Company’s Form 10-K For The Fiscal Year Ended November 30, 1999, on September 18, 1998, the District Court ordered that the three actions be consolidated, appointed a group of lead plaintiffs in the consolidated actions, approved the lead plaintiffs selection of counsel for the purported class in the consolidated actions, and directed the lead plaintiffs to file a consolidated complaint. The consolidated and amended class action complaint ("Consolidated Complaint") which has been filed asserts the same legal claims based on essentially the same factual allegations as did the Complaints. On February 19, 1999, the Company and the Individual Defendants filed a motion to dismiss the Consolidated Complaint. The District Court has denied the motion to dismiss. The Company and the Individual Defendants have answered the Consolidated Complaint, denying all liability and raising various affirmative defenses. Discovery has commenced.
The original complaint alleges that defendants misrepresented, beginning July 15, 1997, that Quintel had achieved record operating results for the quarter ended May 31, 1997, as a result of improved collection procedures from customers of its psychic telephone hotline, and Quintel's expanded business relationship with AT&T. In fact, defendants knew, during the Class Period, that Quintel was continuing to experience poor collections from customers and would have to increase materially its allowance for reserves to reflect its actual experience with nonpayment, and that AT&T had sharply curtailed its business relationship with Quintel.

risk_card.data_coverage_note: SCA database contains ~6,982 filings (~35% of estimated universe). NERA/Cornerstone are authoritative full-universe references.

screening_questions.question: Has the company disclosed any material weakness or significant deficiency in internal controls in the last 3 years?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Material weaknesses are the #1 precursor to restatements. Restatements shift avg settlement from $48M to $152M — a 3.2x multiplier.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Clean SOX 404 opinions for 3+ consecutive years with no significant deficiencies
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any material weakness disclosed, especially if unremediated. Significant deficiency in revenue recognition or financial close process.

screening_questions.question: Is this a repeat SCA target? How many prior filings, and what were the outcomes?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: 25% of all SCAs involve companies sued before. 46 companies have been sued 4+ times. Prior history is a base-rate multiplier — plaintiffs bar has institutional memory.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No prior SCAs. Or: 1 prior SCA that was dismissed, >5 years ago.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any prior settled SCA. 2+ prior filings regardless of outcome. Active ongoing SCA.

screening_questions.question: Does the company have complex revenue recognition (ASC 606)? Multiple performance obligations, variable consideration, or long-term contracts?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Revenue recognition complexity is the single most common allegation type in accounting fraud SCAs. ASC 606 implementation created new attack surfaces.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Simple revenue model (product ships, revenue recognized). Single performance obligation per contract.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Multiple element arrangements, bill-and-hold, percentage of completion, channel stuffing risk, significant variable consideration

screening_questions.question: Has the stock experienced a >15% single-day drop in the last 12 months?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Stock drops >15% are the mechanical trigger for plaintiffs firms to evaluate filing. Larger drops = larger damages = higher settlement.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No single-day drop >10% in 12 months. Low volatility stock.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any single-day drop >15%. Multiple drops >10%. Stock down >30% from 12-month high.

screening_questions.question: What is the delta between GAAP and non-GAAP earnings? Is the non-GAAP adjustment >20% of GAAP?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Large GAAP/non-GAAP deltas create the "earnings quality" allegations that dominate accounting fraud SCAs. Plaintiffs argue the non-GAAP figures are misleading.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Non-GAAP adjustments <10% of GAAP, limited to standard items (stock comp, amortization)
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Non-GAAP adjustments >20% of GAAP, includes unusual addbacks, company leads investor communications with non-GAAP

screening_questions.question: Has the company experienced a significant operational disruption (supply chain, manufacturing, IT outage, data breach) in the last 12 months?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Operational failures have 61% dismissal rate — better odds for defense — but when they settle, avg is $55M. The CrowdStrike case is the archetype.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No material operational disruptions. Diversified supply chain. Demonstrated business continuity capabilities.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Major disruption disclosed (plant shutdown, supply chain break, IT system failure, product recall). Customer concentration means one disruption hits hard.

screening_questions.question: CFO tenure: how long has the current CFO been in role? Any CFO changes in last 18 months?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: The MSA lesson: a 4-month CFO was missed in diligence. Short CFO tenure correlates with both (a) inherited accounting problems and (b) new CFO discovering and disclosing issues.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: CFO tenure >3 years, no recent changes, prior CFO departed for normal reasons
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: CFO tenure <18 months, especially if predecessor departed abruptly or "to pursue other opportunities"

screening_questions.question: What is the market cap? How does it map to the settlement severity curve for the relevant scenario?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Market cap is the strongest predictor of settlement size — its the damages denominator. A $50B company with a 20% drop has a $10B damages pool.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Market cap <$1B with simple business model — severity likely <$10M even if case settles.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Market cap >$10B — enters the zone where P90 settlements exceed $100M. Tower adequacy becomes critical.

screening_questions.question: Is the auditor a Big 4 firm? Any auditor changes in the last 24 months?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Non-Big 4 auditors correlate with higher restatement risk. Auditor changes mid-cycle are a red flag for disagreements on accounting treatment.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Big 4 auditor, same firm for 5+ years, clean opinions
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Non-Big 4 auditor, or auditor change within 24 months, especially if prior auditor resigned (vs. was replaced)

screening_questions.question: Does the company have customer concentration >20% in any single customer?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Customer concentration amplifies operational failure risk — losing one customer due to operational problems creates both the revenue miss and the stock drop.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No customer >10% of revenue. Diversified customer base across industries.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any customer >20% of revenue. Government contract dependency. Key platform dependency (e.g., single cloud provider).

screening_questions.question: What is short interest as a % of float? Has it increased >50% in the last 90 days?
screening_questions.data_source: FINRA short interest data, short seller report databases
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Rising short interest is a leading indicator of both stock drops and short-seller reports that trigger SCAs (Hercules Capital/HTGC pattern).
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Short interest <3% of float, stable or declining.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Short interest >8% of float, or any increase >50% in 90 days. Named in short seller report.

screening_questions.question: Is the company involved in an announced or rumored M&A transaction (as target or acquirer)?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Merger SCAs dismiss 89% of the time — highest dismissal rate. But they file at high frequency (404 in DB). Price for defense costs, not indemnity.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No M&A activity. Company is not in a sector with active consolidation.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Active M&A: announced deal, going-private transaction, activist investor pushing for sale. Acquirer paying significant premium.
